summary: O'Neil reflects on why science fiction never appealed to her growing up as the daughter of sci-fi-loving atheist nerds. She identifies the genre's adolescent male fantasy tropes—brilliant misunderstood men, stupid but gorgeous women who exist to be conquered—as fundamentally alienating to female readers. Using a memorably absurd Heinlein quote as evidence, she explains how boys could identify with these narratives while girls faced impossible choices: deny their gender or prove themselves exceptions to the 'bimbo' rule. The post concludes warmly, noting that she and her husband now mock these tropes together.
key_points: Classic sci-fi was dominated by adolescent male power fantasies with minimal actual science
Female readers faced a lose-lose proposition: either deny their gender or constantly prove they weren't like 'those women'
Male readers could genuinely identify with protagonists in ways that were impossible for women
The genre's treatment of women—as stupid, vile obstacles to be sexually conquered—made sustained interest impossible for her
Progress exists: her husband can now laugh at the ridiculous posturing he once identified with
money_quotes: I felt warm and relaxed, as if I had just killed a man or had a woman.
Too much getting laid and proving yourself to other men, too little science. Waaay too little science.
Most of the time, the set-up seemed along these lines: some extremely macho guy, misunderstood and brilliant, gets into some kind of jam and uses his brilliant mind to find his way out of it.
When I read it, I naturally concluded that it wasn't about me at all, that it was in fact alien to me.
Either of these attitudes, both of which I tried on at different times, were and are fucked up.
Unlike those bimbos who couldn't even fix a broken warp drive, I would be different. I'd have deep thoughts too, just like men.
key_insight: Genre fiction that centers male power fantasies forces female readers into psychologically damaging choices—erasure or exceptionalism—neither of which allows genuine engagement with the work.
